option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Project Structure Guide
This document explains the organization of the Data Warehouse & DM Management project.
## Directory Organization
### `/sql` - SQL Scripts & Database Objects
Contains all database initialization and operation scripts. Execute in numerical order:
``` 10_schema.sql # Base schemas & tables for branch databases # Creates: branch_west, branch_east with public schema # Tables: customer, product, category, product_category, sale, sale_item
11_fk.sql # Foreign key constraints # Links: sale→customer, sale_item→sale+product, etc.
20_dwh_schema.sql # DWH (Data Warehouse) schema and tables # Dimensions: dim_branch, dim_customer, dim_product, dim_category, dim_date # Bridge: bridge_product_category # Facts: fact_sale_item
21_fdw.sql # Foreign Data Wrapper configuration # Creates FDW servers: srv_west, srv_east # Imports foreign schemas: src_west, src_east # Enables DWH to query branch databases remotely
22_dwh_load.sql # Initial DWH data population # Loads dimensions from foreign tables # Populates fact_sale_item
30_dm_schema.sql # Data Mart (DM) schema & materialized views # Creates analytical views for reporting
31_dm_load.sql # Initial DM load from DWH
31_dm_load_range.sql # DM load by date range (for updates)
40_dwh_incremental_load.sql # Incremental DWH load mechanism # Handles new branch data updates
60_restore_branch_from_dwh.sql # ⭐ MAIN PROCEDURE # Restores lost branch data from DWH # Creates: restore_branch_from_dwh() procedure # Creates: restore_log table for tracking ```
### `/docker` - Container Configuration
``` docker-compose.yml # Orchestrates all services (db, pgadmin, seeder)
docker/ ├── init/ │ └── 00_full_init.sql # Alternative full initialization script │ └── seeder/ ├── Dockerfile # Python 3.12 image with requirements ├── requirements.txt # Dependencies: psycopg2-binary, Faker ├── .env.seeder # Seeder-specific environment variables └── seed.py # Data generation logic (creates test data) ```
### `/scripts` - Automation & Testing
``` restore_branch_from_dwh_cli.sh # CLI-based recovery (alternative to procedure) # Can run from shell instead of psql
test_restore_lab6.sh # Automated test suite # Tests data recovery scenarios ```
### `/docs` - Documentation & Reports
``` lab1_report.md # Lab 1: Base Schema Design # Covers: branch database structure, tables, relationships
lab2_report.md # Lab 2: Referential Integrity # Covers: FK constraints, cascading rules, data validation
lab3_report.md # Lab 3: DWH Architecture # Covers: dimensional modeling, Kimball approach, schema design
lab4_report.md # Lab 4: Incremental Loading # Covers: ETL patterns, change detection, merging strategies
lab5_report.md # Lab 5: Data Analytics & Reporting # Covers: DM queries, aggregations, business analytics
lab6_report.md # Lab 6: Data Recovery & Restoration ⭐ # Covers: disaster recovery, idempotent procedures, testing
img/ # Screenshots, diagrams, visualizations ├── data_model.png # ER diagram of branch databases ├── kimball.png # DWH dimensional model diagram ├── view.png # Data mart structure diagram └── *.png # Query results, test outputs ```
### `/diagrams` - Architecture & Process Diagrams
PlantUML source files for system visualization:
``` data_model.puml # Entity-Relationship Diagram (ERD) # Shows all tables and relationships
kimball.puml # Dimensional Model Diagram # Shows DWH star schema structure
view.puml # Data Mart Views Diagram # Shows analytical query layers
dm_load_week_sales_range.puml # ETL Process Flow # Shows data movement and transformations ```
### Root Level Files
``` README.md # Project overview & quick start guide # Main entry point for portfolio viewers
.env # Environment variables # Database credentials, PGDATA path
docker-compose.yml # Docker services orchestration # All service definitions
.gitignore # Git exclusions # Excludes: .env, volumes, IDE files ```
## Execution Workflow
### Phase 1: Environment Setup ```bash docker compose down -v # Clean start docker compose up -d --build # Build & start services docker compose logs -f seeder # Monitor seeding ```
### Phase 2: DWH Initialization (Sequential) ```bash # Step 1: Create DWH structure docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh -f /docker/sql/20_dwh_schema.sql
# Step 2: Configure FDW docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh -f /docker/sql/21_fdw.sql
# Step 3: Load DWH data docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh -f /docker/sql/22_dwh_load.sql
# Step 4: Create extensions docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh \ -c "CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pgcrypto;"
# Step 5: Create restore procedure docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh -f /docker/sql/60_restore_branch_from_dwh.sql ```
### Phase 3: Data Recovery Testing ```bash # Clear target branch docker compose exec -T b psql -U postgres -d branch_west \ -c "TRUNCATE sale_item, sale, product_category, product, category, customer RESTART IDENTITY;"
# Execute recovery docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh \ -c "CALL restore_branch_from_dwh('west', '2025-01-01'::date, '2025-11-19'::date);"
# Verify results docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh \ -c "SELECT * FROM dwh.restore_log ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 1;" ```
## Data Flow Architecture
``` ┌──────────────────────┐ │ Seed Data (Faker) │ │ ↓ │ │ branch_west DB │ │ branch_east DB │ └──────────────────────┘ │ ↓ (21_fdw.sql creates FDW) ┌──────────────────────┐ │ DWH (dwh) │ │ Dimensions + Facts │ │ + restore_log │ └──────────────────────┘ │ ├─→ (30-31_dm_load) │ Data Mart (DM) │ └─→ (60_restore) ⭐ Recovery to branch (if data lost) ```
## Key Tables & Schemas
### Branch Databases (branch_west, branch_east)
**public schema:** - `customer` (customer_id, customer_name, email, ...) - `product` (product_id, product_name, list_price, ...) - `category` (category_id, category_name, ...) - `product_category` (product_id, category_id) - `sale` (sale_id, customer_id, sale_date, total_amount, ...) - `sale_item` (sale_item_id, sale_id, product_id, quantity, ...)
### DWH Database (dwh)
**dwh schema:** - `dim_branch` (branch_key, branch_code, branch_name) - `dim_customer` (customer_key, customer_id, customer_name, src_id, ...) - `dim_product` (product_key, product_id, product_name, list_price, ...) - `dim_category` (category_key, category_id, category_name, ...) - `dim_date` (date_key, full_date, year, month, day, ...) - `bridge_product_category` (bridge_key, product_key, category_key) - `fact_sale_item` (fact_key, sale_key, product_key, customer_key, date_key, branch_key, ...) - `restore_log` (id, branch_code, start_date, end_date, inserted counts, run_at)
**src_west, src_east schemas:** - Foreign tables pointing to remote public schema tables
### Data Mart (DM)
**dm schema:** (Created by 30_dm_schema.sql) - Materialized views for reporting - Aggregated metrics by dimension - Performance-optimized for BI queries
## Environment Variables
Configured in `.env`: ``` POSTGRES_USER=postgres POSTGRES_PASSWORD=... POSTGRES_DB=postgres PGDATA=/var/lib/postgresql/data POSTGRES_IMAGE=postgres:15-alpine ```
## Performance Optimization Notes
1. **Indexes:** FK columns, natural keys 2. **Statistics:** Updated after bulk loads 3. **Partitioning:** fact_sale_item can be partitioned by date 4. **FDW Optimization:** Push predicates to remote servers 5. **VACUUM:** Automatic maintenance enabled
## Testing & Validation Checklist
- [ ] All SQL scripts execute without errors - [ ] DWH contains correct row counts - [ ] FDW can access remote tables - [ ] Restore procedure executes successfully - [ ] Recovered data matches DWH totals - [ ] Recovery log recorded correctly - [ ] Idempotency verified (repeat run gives same result) - [ ] No orphaned foreign keys
## Common Commands Reference
```bash # View logs docker compose logs -f seeder
# Access database directly docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh
# Run SQL file docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh -f /docker/sql/file.sql
# Execute single SQL command docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh -c "SELECT 1;"
# List databases docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -l
# Connect to specific DB and describe table docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d branch_west -c "\d sale"
# Show FDW status docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -d dwh -c "\des+"
# Monitor active queries docker compose exec -T db psql -U postgres -c " SELECT * FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state != 'idle'; " ```
---
**For detailed information about each lab/component, see the respective documentation in `/docs`.**

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Technical Highlights & Architecture
A detailed technical breakdown of the Data Warehouse & DM Management System for portfolio evaluation.
## Educational Value & Skills Demonstrated
### 1. **Database Design & Modeling**
#### Relational Schema (Branch Databases) - **Normalization:** 3NF normalization applied - **PK/FK Design:** Surrogate keys (IDENTITY) + Natural keys - **Constraints:** NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK constraints - **Cascading:** ON DELETE RESTRICT for referential integrity
**Key Tables:** ```sql -- customer (Master table) CREATE TABLE customer ( customer_id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_name TEXT NOT NULL, email TEXT, phone TEXT, rowguid UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), modifieddate TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP );
-- product (Master table) CREATE TABLE product ( product_id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY, product_name TEXT NOT NULL, list_price NUMERIC(10,2), category_id BIGINT, rowguid UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), modifieddate TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP );
-- sale (Transaction header) CREATE TABLE sale ( sale_id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_id BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES customer(customer_id), sale_date DATE NOT NULL, total_amount NUMERIC(12,2), rowguid UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), modifieddate TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP );
-- sale_item (Transaction detail) CREATE TABLE sale_item ( sale_item_id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY, sale_id BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES sale(sale_id), product_id BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES product(product_id), quantity NUMERIC(10,3), unit_price NUMERIC(10,2), line_amount NUMERIC(12,2), rowguid UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), modifieddate TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); ```
#### Dimensional Model (DWH - Kimball Approach) - **Star Schema:** Fact table surrounded by dimension tables - **Conformed Dimensions:** Single source of truth for dimensions - **Role-Playing:** dim_date reused for different roles - **Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD):** Type 1 (overwrite) applied - **Bridges:** Many-to-many relationships via bridge tables
**Schema Structure:** ``` dim_branch ↑ | dim_customer ← fact_sale_item → dim_product ← bridge_product_category → dim_category ↓ dim_date ```
### 2. **Advanced SQL Techniques**
#### Stored Procedures with Complex Logic
**restore_branch_from_dwh() Procedure** - **Idempotent Design:** Safe to run multiple times - **Temporary Tables:** For ID mapping during restoration - **Natural Key Matching:** Prevents duplicates via UPSERT pattern - **Transaction Isolation:** SAVEPOINT for error handling - **Bulk Operations:** Efficient batch INSERT/UPDATE
```sql -- Simplified example of key logic CREATE TEMP TABLE IF NOT EXISTS temp_customer_map ( src_customer_id BIGINT, tgt_customer_id BIGINT );
-- Natural key matching for idempotency INSERT INTO temp_customer_map (src_customer_id, tgt_customer_id) SELECT dc.customer_id, c.customer_id FROM dwh.dim_customer dc LEFT JOIN src_west.customer c ON c.customer_name = dc.customer_name WHERE EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM dwh.fact_sale_item f WHERE f.customer_key = dc.customer_key AND ...date range filter... );
-- Restore missing customers INSERT INTO src_west.customer (customer_name, email, phone, rowguid, modifieddate) SELECT dc.customer_name, dc.email, dc.phone, gen_random_uuid(), NOW() FROM dwh.dim_customer dc WHERE NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM src_west.customer c WHERE c.customer_name = dc.customer_name ) AND EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM dwh.fact_sale_item f WHERE f.customer_key = dc.customer_key AND ...date range filter... ); ```
#### Window Functions & Aggregations - Used in data mart queries for ranking, running totals - ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK() for top-N queries - LAG(), LEAD() for trend analysis - Aggregate functions with OVER clause
#### Common Table Expressions (CTEs) - WITH clauses for query modularity - Recursive CTEs for hierarchical data (if used) - Multiple CTEs for complex transformations
#### Foreign Data Wrapper (FDW) Queries - Cross-database queries without data duplication - Transparent access to remote tables - Query pushdown to remote servers
```sql -- FDW example: DWH queries branch data seamlessly SELECT COUNT(*) FROM src_west.sale s JOIN dwh.dim_customer dc ON dc.customer_id = s.customer_id; ```
### 3. **Data Integration & ETL**
#### Python Data Seeding - **Faker Library:** Generate realistic test data - **Database Connectivity:** psycopg2 for PostgreSQL - **Batch Operations:** Efficient bulk inserts - **Error Handling:** Transaction rollback on failure
```python # Simplified seed.py logic from faker import Faker import psycopg2
def seed_branch_data(branch_code): faker = Faker() conn = psycopg2.connect(dbname=f"branch_{branch_code}", ...)
# Generate 30 customers for _ in range(30): cursor.execute( "INSERT INTO customer (customer_name, email) VALUES (%s, %s)", (faker.name(), faker.email()) )
# Generate 50 sales with items customers = cursor.execute("SELECT customer_id FROM customer") for _ in range(50): customer_id = random.choice(customers) sale_id = cursor.execute( "INSERT INTO sale (customer_id, sale_date, total_amount) " "VALUES (%s, %s, %s) RETURNING sale_id", (customer_id, faker.date(), faker.pydecimal(10, 2)) )[0][0]
# Insert 3-5 items per sale for _ in range(random.randint(3, 5)): cursor.execute( "INSERT INTO sale_item (...) VALUES (...)" )
conn.commit() ```
### 4. **Distributed Database Architecture**
#### Multi-Database System - **3 Databases:** branch_west, branch_east, dwh - **Foreign Data Wrapper:** Enables DWH to access branch data - **Network Topology:** Docker network (internal communication) - **Data Synchronization:** One-directional (branches → DWH)
#### Database Replication Pattern - Branches are source systems (operational databases) - DWH is aggregation layer (reporting/analytics) - Unidirectional data flow prevents circular dependencies
``` branch_west (source) ──┐ │ FDW/ETL branch_east (source) ──┤──→ DWH (aggregation) │ time-based ```
### 5. **DevOps & Containerization**
#### Docker Compose Orchestration ```yaml services: db: image: postgres:15-alpine volumes: - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data # Persistent storage - ./sql:/docker/sql:ro # SQL scripts (RO) healthcheck: # Service dependency test: pg_isready retries: 20
pgadmin: image: dpage/pgadmin4 depends_on: [db]
seeder: image: python:3.12-slim command: pip install -r requirements.txt && python seed.py depends_on: db: condition: service_healthy ```
**Key DevOps Concepts:** - ✅ Image versioning (alpine for minimal size) - ✅ Volume management (persistent + read-only) - ✅ Service dependencies & health checks - ✅ Environment configuration (.env) - ✅ Isolated networks (Docker networking)
### 6. **Data Recovery & Disaster Recovery**
#### Idempotent Procedure Design - **Problem:** Repeated executions must produce identical results - **Solution:** Natural key matching + upsert logic
**Idempotency Guarantee:** ``` 1st run: inserts 30 customers → restore_log records 30 2nd run: finds existing 30 customers → inserts 0 → restore_log records 0 3rd run: same as 2nd run ```
#### Recovery Workflow 1. **Identify Lost Data:** By branch_code and date range 2. **Extract from DWH:** Via FDW or direct query 3. **Map IDs:** Natural keys ensure correct ID assignment 4. **Restore:** INSERT with conflict avoidance 5. **Log:** Record metrics in restore_log table 6. **Verify:** Reconciliation query compares sums
#### Logging & Audit Trail ```sql CREATE TABLE dwh.restore_log ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, branch_code TEXT, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, customers_inserted INT, products_inserted INT, sales_inserted INT, sale_items_inserted INT, run_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW() ); ```
### 7. **Performance Optimization**
#### Indexing Strategy ```sql -- FK columns indexed automatically by PG CREATE INDEX idx_sale_customer_id ON sale(customer_id);
-- Natural key indexes for lookups CREATE INDEX idx_customer_name ON customer(customer_name); CREATE INDEX idx_product_name ON product(product_name);
-- Date range indexes (for time-series queries) CREATE INDEX idx_sale_date ON sale(sale_date);
-- Composite indexes for joins CREATE INDEX idx_sale_item_sale_product ON sale_item(sale_id, product_id); ```
#### Query Optimization - **EXPLAIN ANALYZE:** Used for query plan analysis - **Statistics:** ANALYZE run after bulk operations - **Vacuum:** Cleanup dead tuples and update statistics - **Partition Pruning:** fact_sale_item can be partitioned by year
**Example EXPLAIN output interpretation:** ``` Seq Scan on sale s (cost=0.00..250.00 rows=100) Filter: (sale_date > '2025-01-01') → pushdown predicate → Index scan would be faster if index exists ```
#### Materialized Views for Analytics ```sql CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW dm.sales_summary AS SELECT d.full_date, b.branch_code, SUM(f.line_amount) as daily_sales, COUNT(DISTINCT f.sale_key) as transaction_count FROM dwh.fact_sale_item f JOIN dwh.dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key JOIN dwh.dim_branch b ON f.branch_key = b.branch_key GROUP BY d.full_date, b.branch_code;
CREATE INDEX idx_sales_summary_date ON dm.sales_summary(full_date); ```
### 8. **Data Quality & Validation**
#### Constraints Enforcement - **Primary Keys:** Uniqueness & NOT NULL - **Foreign Keys:** Referential integrity with ON DELETE RESTRICT - **Check Constraints:** Domain validation (prices >= 0, quantities > 0) - **Unique Constraints:** Natural key protection
#### Reconciliation Queries ```sql -- Verify recovered data matches DWH SELECT 'DWH' as source, SUM(line_amount) as total, COUNT(*) as item_count FROM dwh.fact_sale_item f WHERE branch_key = (SELECT branch_key FROM dwh.dim_branch WHERE branch_code='west')
UNION ALL
SELECT 'branch_west' as source, SUM(si.line_amount), COUNT(*) FROM branch_west.sale_item si; ```
## Portfolio Strengths
### For Recruiters: 1. **Full-Stack Data Engineering:** DB design to application layer 2. **Enterprise Patterns:** FDW, dimension modeling, idempotency 3. **Production-Ready:** Error handling, logging, validation 4. **DevOps Integration:** Docker, CI/CD ready structure 5. **Documentation:** Comprehensive reports & diagrams
### For Interviews: - **Design Decisions:** Can explain why Star schema vs 3NF - **Trade-offs:** FDW vs ETL, materialized views vs queries - **Scalability:** Partitioning, replication, sharding approaches - **Data Quality:** Constraints, reconciliation, validation
### For Learning: - **PostgreSQL Deep Dive:** Advanced features (FDW, procedures, UPSERT) - **SQL Mastery:** Complex queries, window functions, CTEs - **Systems Thinking:** Multi-tier architecture, data flow design - **Best Practices:** Idempotency, testing, documentation
## Complexity Analysis
| Component | Complexity | Notes | |-----------|-----------|-------| | Branch Schema | O(1) | Simple 6-table design | | DWH Loading | O(n) | Linear time, n = fact rows | | ID Mapping | O(n log n) | Hash join with sorting | | Recovery | O(n*m) | n=dimensions, m=facts in range | | FDW Queries | O(n+m) | Network latency + execution |
**Scalability:** - Current: ~300 facts per branch, works instantly - Potential: 100M facts with proper partitioning - Bottleneck: FDW network latency (solved by caching)
## Test Coverage
**Scenarios Tested:** 1. ✅ Fresh database initialization 2. ✅ Data seeding completeness 3. ✅ DWH aggregation accuracy 4. ✅ FDW connectivity & pushdown 5. ✅ Recovery with empty target 6. ✅ Recovery with partial data 7. ✅ Idempotency (repeated runs) 8. ✅ Data reconciliation (sums match) 9. ✅ Referential integrity 10. ✅ Constraint enforcement
## Key Learning Outcomes
After studying this project, you'll understand:
- [ ] How star schema differs from normalized OLTP schema - [ ] Role of Foreign Data Wrapper in distributed systems - [ ] Idempotent design patterns for automation - [ ] Natural vs surrogate keys trade-offs - [ ] ETL pipeline design & execution - [ ] Bulk data loading best practices - [ ] Docker compose for multi-service orchestration - [ ] SQL procedure development & testing - [ ] Data reconciliation & validation techniques - [ ] Query optimization & index strategies
---
**This project demonstrates enterprise-level data engineering skills suitable for mid-senior positions.**
